title: Small talk â€” topics and reactions
description: Start and sustain small talk conversations about common topics â€” weather, sport, food, weekend plans, and current events.

content.title: What is small talk and why does it matter?
content.body:
SMALL TALK is light, casual conversation about everyday topics. It's important because:
â€¢ It builds relationships
â€¢ It breaks the ice with new people
â€¢ It fills comfortable silences
â€¢ It's the first step to deeper conversations

COMMON SMALL TALK TOPICS:
âœ“ Weather: 'Can you believe this heat?'
âœ“ Weekend plans: 'Any plans this weekend?'
âœ“ Food: 'Have you tried the new place on the corner?'
âœ“ Work (general): 'How's work going?'
âœ“ Sport: 'Did you watch the match last night?'
âœ“ Travel: 'Have you been anywhere nice recently?'
âœ“ Current events (positive/neutral): 'Did you see the festival in the city?'

AVOID for first small talk:
âœ— Money, salary, politics (controversial)
âœ— Personal family problems
âœ— Negative complaints about people
content.grammar_note:
Small talk uses present simple, present perfect (Have you...?), and past simple
Questions are open-ended to encourage the other person to talk

content.title: Reaction phrases and keeping conversation going
content.body:
Show you're listening and engaged:

REACTIONS:
â€¢ Really? / Oh wow!
â€¢ That sounds lovely / That sounds tough!
â€¢ How interesting!
â€¢ You're kidding!
â€¢ Tell me more!
â€¢ I can imagine.
â€¢ Oh no! / That's great!

FOLLOW-UP QUESTIONS to keep talking:
â€¢ 'What was it like?'
â€¢ 'How long were you there?'
â€¢ 'Did you enjoy it?'
â€¢ 'Would you recommend it?'
â€¢ 'What do you think of it?'
â€¢ 'Was it your first time?'
content.grammar_note:
Reactions show you're actively listening
Follow-up questions are WH- or YES/NO questions
Avoid one-word answers in small talk

content.title: Vocabulary: Small talk topics in Indian context

vocabulary.example: It's incredibly humid in Chennai right now!
How's your commute? The traffic on that road is terrible.

vocabulary.meaning: Indian festivals â€” good neutral small talk topics
vocabulary.example: Any plans for Diwali this year?

vocabulary.meaning: Indian Premier League â€” cricket tournament
vocabulary.example: Did you watch the IPL final last night?

vocabulary.meaning: a holiday spent at or near home
vocabulary.example: We had a staycation this weekend â€” explored our own city.
I'm a total foodie â€” I try a new restaurant every weekend.

sentences: Can you believe this rain? It's been non-stop since Monday!
Did you watch the IPL final last night? What a match!
Any plans for the long weekend? Going anywhere?
Have you tried that new restaurant on Anna Salai? I heard it's amazing.
Oh wow â€” that sounds wonderful! How long were you there?

content.question: You're in a lift with a colleague you don't know well. What's the best small talk opener?
options: Can you believe how hot it's been this week?
Why did your team not do well last quarter?
content.correct: Can you believe how hot it's been this week?
content.explanation: Weather is the classic safe small talk topic â€” neutral, relatable, and non-controversial.

content.question: Your colleague says: 'I went to Coorg last weekend!' What's the best response?
options: Oh wow â€” Coorg is beautiful! How long were you there? Was the weather good?
content.correct: Oh wow â€” Coorg is beautiful! How long were you there? Was the weather good?
content.explanation: Show genuine interest with a reaction + follow-up question. This keeps the conversation alive.

content.question: Which topic is MOST suitable for small talk with a new colleague?
options: Whether they have any plans for the upcoming long weekend
Why they left their last company
content.correct: Whether they have any plans for the upcoming long weekend
content.explanation: Weekend plans = safe, positive, and invites a friendly conversation without personal or controversial topics.

content.question: Your friend mentions they tried a new restaurant. What follow-up question keeps the conversation going?
options: How was it? What did you order? Would you recommend it?
content.correct: How was it? What did you order? Would you recommend it?
content.explanation: Multiple relevant follow-up questions show genuine interest and keep the conversation flowing.

content.instruction: Complete the small talk conversation with a natural reaction and follow-up.

sentences.text: 'I went to Pondicherry last weekend!' â†’ '___ ___ â€” was it your first time there?'
'I've been learning to play the guitar.' â†’ '___ interesting! How long have you been learning?'
'The traffic was terrible today â€” two hours to get here!' â†’ '___ no â€” that must have been exhausting!'
'I tried making biryani for the first time this weekend.' â†’ '___ you? How did it turn out?'

content.instruction: Choose the best small talk topic opener for each situation.

sentences.text: Meeting a colleague on a Monday morning: '___ did you get up to this weekend?' (What)
Waiting for a delayed train with a stranger: 'Can you ___ how delayed this train is?' (believe)
After a cricket match everyone was watching: '___ you watch the match last night?' (Did)
During the festival season: '___ plans for Diwali?' (Any)

content.instruction: Arrange into a small talk opener.
content.answer: Can you believe the heat this week?
Was it your first time there?

content.instruction: Match the small talk statement to the best reaction + follow-up.

pairs.right: Oh wow â€” congratulations! What's the new role?
Oh no! How long were you stuck in traffic?
You're kidding! Was it terrifying? Would you do it again?

pairs.left: 'I've started cooking South Indian food at home.'
pairs.right: Oh how lovely! What have you made so far?
Oh amazing â€” is this your first time? How long are you going for?

content.title: Small talk at a wedding â€” Hyderabad
content.setting: Priya and Sonal meet at a mutual friend's wedding in Hyderabad. They don't know each other well â€” small talk brings them together.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: It's a beautiful wedding, isn't it? The decorations are stunning!

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Absolutely gorgeous! They've done such a wonderful job. Are you a friend of the bride or the groom?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: College friend of the bride! We've known each other for almost ten years. You?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I'm the groom's cousin â€” I've come all the way from Bangalore for this. Have you been to Hyderabad before?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: A couple of times but never long enough to really explore. Anything you'd recommend?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Oh you must try the biryani here â€” it's on a completely different level! Not to take anything away from other cities, but Hyderabadi biryani is something else.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Ha! I completely agree â€” we had some last night and it was incredible. Are you a foodie?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Total foodie! Wherever I travel, trying local food is number one on the list. What about you â€” do you like to travel?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Love it! Though I haven't gone anywhere in a while. I went to Coorg about three months ago â€” that was beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Oh, I've been wanting to go there! How was it? Was it very crowded?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Not at all â€” we went on a weekday which helped. The coffee estates are just breathtaking. You should definitely go!

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I will! This has been so lovely â€” it's refreshing to actually have a proper conversation at these events. Most people are just on their phones!

comprehension_questions.question: How does Priya know the couple getting married?
options: She is a college friend of the bride.
comprehension_questions.correct: She is a college friend of the bride.

comprehension_questions.question: What does Sonal say is the most important thing when she travels?
Where did Priya go three months ago?

content.instruction: Find the small talk mistake â€” these responses kill the conversation.

sentences.wrong: Friend: 'I went to Shimla last weekend!' You: 'Nice.'
sentences.correct: Friend: 'I went to Shimla last weekend!' You: 'Oh, how amazing! Was it cold? What was the best part of the trip?'
sentences.explanation: One-word responses end conversations. Show genuine interest with a reaction + follow-up questions.

sentences.wrong: Colleague: 'Any plans for the weekend?' You: 'No.'
sentences.correct: Colleague: 'Any plans for the weekend?' You: 'Not much planned honestly â€” maybe a quiet weekend at home. What about you?'
sentences.explanation: Even if you have no plans, give a brief answer and return the question. 'No' alone is too curt.

sentences.wrong: Starting small talk: 'So â€” what's your salary at this company?'
sentences.correct: Starting small talk: 'Have you been here long? How are you finding the company so far?'
sentences.explanation: Salary is too personal for small talk with new people. Start with safe, general topics.

content.scenario: You're at a colleague's farewell party. You don't know most people there. Someone stands next to you at the food table. What do you say to start a small talk conversation?
options: Do you know how much this event cost?
Great spread, isn't it? Have you tried the paneer?
I don't really know anyone here.
content.correct: Great spread, isn't it? Have you tried the paneer?
content.explanation: Food at an event = perfect small talk opener. Light, positive, relatable, and opens the door for a reply.

content.instruction: Practise starting small talk in 3 different situations. For each one, give an opening sentence AND a follow-up question after the person responds.
content.prompt: Start small talk in: (1) a company lift on a Monday, (2) while waiting for a delayed train, (3) at a friend's birthday party with new people.
content.example_response:
Situation 1 â€” Lift on a Monday morning: 'Morning! Can you believe the traffic today? I was stuck near the signal for almost 40 minutes! Is your commute usually this bad?' If they say yes: 'Oh no â€” which route do you take? I should probably try the metro more often honestly!'

Situation 2 â€” Delayed train: 'This delay is something else, isn't it? I've been here 30 minutes already. Are you travelling far? At least the platform isn't too crowded!' If they say they're going to Coimbatore: 'Oh lovely â€” is it a holiday trip or visiting family?'

Situation 3 â€” Birthday party: 'Great party, isn't it? The food is amazing â€” have you tried that mango dessert? I think I've had three already! Are you a friend of Meera's from work or college?' If they say college: 'Oh nice! Which college? I was in Bangalore for three years myself â€” whereabouts?'

content.instruction: Think about the best small talk conversation you've ever had â€” or imagine one. What was the topic? How did it start? Where did it go? What made it so enjoyable?
content.prompt: Describe a memorable small talk conversation â€” real or imagined â€” that flowed really well.
content.example_response: One of the best small talk conversations I've ever had was completely unexpected. I was waiting at a bus stop in Chennai during a sudden downpour â€” absolutely soaked and a bit annoyed. An elderly gentleman next to me said, 'Well, this rain has no manners, does it?' â€” which made me laugh immediately. We started talking about the monsoon and how unpredictable the Chennai rains are. He told me he'd lived in the city for over 60 years and could predict rain by looking at the sea. I was fascinated. It led to a conversation about how the city had changed â€” the old Madras versus new Chennai. He knew so many beautiful stories about the old neighbourhood markets, the old cinema halls, the filter coffee culture. By the time the bus came, almost 20 minutes had passed and I was genuinely sad to end the conversation. What made it special was that it started with something simple â€” the weather â€” and turned into this rich, unexpected exchange. The best small talk always does that.

content.instruction: Complete this small talk conversation at an office networking event.
content.text:
Ravi: Hi! First time at one of these events?

Priya: Yes actually! A bit ___ (1) but everyone seems friendly. You?

Ravi: I've been ___ (2) a couple of times. Gets easier â€” the food alone makes it worth it! ___ (3) you tried the kebabs?

Priya: Not yet â€” are they good? I'll have to ___ (4) them! Have you worked in Bangalore long?

Ravi: About three years now. Moved here from Hyderabad. It's a great city â€” do you ___ (5) it here?

Priya: I love it! The weather is what did it for me â€” after Chennai summers, Bangalore feels ___ (6) paradise! Where in Hyderabad are you from?

Ravi: Banjara Hills area. Beautiful but ___ (7) Mumbai levels of traffic. Anyway â€” shall we grab some of those kebabs before they disappear?

Priya: Absolutely â€” ___ (8) now while we can!

content.title: Lesson 8 complete â€” Small talk
key_points: Safe topics: weather, food, weekend, travel, sport, festivals â€” NOT salary, politics, or personal problems
Open with relatable openers: 'Can you believeâ€¦?', 'Have you triedâ€¦?', 'Any plans forâ€¦?'
React with interest: 'Oh wow!', 'Really?', 'That sounds amazing!', 'You're kidding!'
Follow-up questions keep the conversation alive: 'How was it?', 'Would you recommend it?'
Return the question: after answering, ask back â€” 'What about you?'
Never give one-word answers in small talk â€” always add something
